cases.notes: Classic anti-pattern: single worker fn, tid-indexed array, struct size 8B < 64B. Tier-1 H2 must detect.
Manual char padding[56] makes struct 64B. H2 size < 64 check fails. H4 sz%64==0 check also clean.
__attribute__((aligned(64))) causes clang to emit { i64, [56 x i8] } in LLVM IR, making struct size 64B. Both H2 and H4 must stay quiet.
Single struct instance, two separate thread entry fns each writing a different field. Both fields at offsets 0 and 8 (bucket 0). H1 must detect.
Fields a (offset 0, bucket 0) and b (offset 64, bucket 1) pushed to different cache lines via char pad[56]. H1 per-bucket unique_fields < 2.

Adjacent _Atomic long fields in same struct, different threads. Tier-2 fires H3/HIGH (severity matches label now; was mislabeled MEDIUM). H3 is a known Tier-1 gap: atomicrmw not matched, H3 not implemented there.
Two adjacent global longs, different threads. H5 requires linker map. Also: store to @global not matched by store_re (needs %reg). Known Tier-1 gap. Verdict will be MISS.

Variable-index array access but NO pthread_create. H2 correctly skips (no thread-reachable fns). H4 also stays quiet: both tiers guard H4 on thread reachability (guard added after this case exposed FPs in round 1).
Threads read adjacent long values via plain ptr, no struct GEPs in thread fn. No store to shared mem. H1/H2/H4 all quiet. Demonstrates readers do not trigger false-sharing heuristics.

pthread_create called with fn loaded into a variable. IR has ptr %fn_var (register), not ptr @worker (literal). Analyzer pthread_re cannot extract entry name. thread_entries=[]. H2 missed (GAP). H4 may fire as side-effect of H4 no-thread-context weakness.

Lock-call modeling (both tiers): pthread_mutex_lock/unlock(&p->m) counts as a write to the lock-word field, so H1 sees m (offset 0) + counter (offset 40) in bucket 0 and fires. Was a labeled opaque-call GAP before the synchronization-modeling round.
SPSC ring: head at offset 0 (producer), tail at offset 8 (consumer). Both in bucket 0. H1 fires on both tier1 and tier2. Classic LMAX Disruptor anti-pattern.

lshaz TrackingStatistic pattern. stat_t is 16B, 4 per cache line, tid-indexed. Initially mislabeled as atomicrmw gap; real root cause was GEP shape: global fixed array emits 'getelementptr [4 x %struct.stat_t], ptr @stats, i64 0, i64 %var' (ArrayType source, not StructType). H2 fixed in both tiers to handle array-of-struct GEPs. Scalar arrays ([8 x i64]) deliberately excluded (H6 territory).

Both tiers MISS. Thread A writes o->x (offset 0 in outer), Thread B writes o->in.b (absolute offset 16). H1 traverses top-level struct fields; nested GEP chain for o->in.b is attributed to struct inner, not outer. Cross-struct field attribution via nested GEP resolution is not implemented. GAP: multi-level nested struct field access not resolved to parent struct bucket.

Struct has pad[16] field that looks like padding but total is only 24B (< 64B). H2 fires on both tiers (24B < 64B, tid-indexed array). 2 elements per cache line. Validates that H2 is not fooled by undersized padding fields.

Each thread mallocs its own private_t (8B struct) inside the thread fn. No global, no shared struct. Both tiers correctly emit no findings. Heap allocation inside thread fn is not visible as cross-thread shared memory. True negative confirmed.

_Alignas(64) on each field causes clang to emit padding bytes: aligned_pair = { i64, [56 x i8], i64, [56 x i8] }, size 128B. Field a at offset 0 (bucket 0), field b at offset 64 (bucket 1). H1 correctly sees different buckets and emits no finding. Both tiers clean. Offset math precision test passed.

Plain global long counters[8], no struct. Thread i writes counters[tid]++. 8 longs = 64B, all on one cache line. H6 (implemented in both tiers after the Huron round showed this is the dominant real-world pattern): variable-index store into shared scalar array, element size < 64B, thread-reachable.

Heap int array via global pointer, thread i writes counters[tid]++. The dominant Huron-suite shape (false.c, locked, lockless, lu_ncb). H6 must fire on heap/pointer bases, not just fixed global arrays.

Thread mallocs own two-field scratch struct and writes both fields (same 64B bucket). Regression test for the H1 instance-privacy fix (Huron lu_ncb LocalCopies FP): pointer born from malloc inside the thread fn, never stored outside local allocas, never passed to pthread_create, never returned -> instance is thread-private, H1 must stay quiet.

Huron histogram mechanism: per-thread arg struct of 104B (104 % 64 = 40), pthread_create hands &args[i] to each thread. Each thread writes only its own element but neighbouring elements straddle shared boundary lines. H7 checks the pthread_create arg GEP: variable index, sizeof >= 64, sizeof % 64 != 0, align < 64.

Threads variable-index a 4B point_t array but only load from it (Phoenix linear_regression POINT_T FP from the Huron run). Regression test for the H2/H4 write requirement: no store through the variable-index GEP chain -> no finding. Result returned via padded per-thread arg struct so H6 stays quiet too.

Interprocedural privacy regression test (Huron lu_ncb LocalCopies FP, second half): thread mallocs private struct, passes it to a static helper that writes both same-bucket fields. Helper's param is private because every call site passes a private value and the helper's address is never taken. H1 must stay quiet in both tiers.
